bibliographic_data.title: Port Contention for Fun and Profit
bibliographic_data.abstract: Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT) architectures are attractive targets for side-channel enabled attackers, with their inherently broader attack surface that exposes more per physical core microarchitecture components than cross-core attacks. In this work, we explore SMT execution engine sharing as a side-channel leakage source. We target ports to stacks of execution units to create a high-resolution timing side-channel due to port contention, inherently stealthy since it does not depend on the memory subsystem like other cache or TLB based attacks. Implementing our channel on Intel Skylake and Kaby Lake architectures featuring Hyper-Threading, we mount an end-to-end attack that recovers a P-384 private key from an OpenSSL-powered TLS server using a small number of repeated TLS handshake attempts. Furthermore, we show that traces targeting shared libraries, static builds, and SGX enclaves are essentially identical, hence our channel has wide target application.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents a new side-channel attack vector called PORTSMASH that exploits port contention in SMT architectures to recover private keys from TLS servers and SGX enclaves.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors target ports of execution units to create a high-resolution timing side-channel due to port contention, which is inherently stealthy as it does not depend on the memory subsystem like other cache or TLB based attacks. The technique can be applied across different scenarios and architectures featuring Hyper-Threading.
outcomes: Recovery of P-384 private key from an OpenSSL-powered TLS server using a small number of repeated TLS handshake attempts.
Demonstration that traces targeting shared libraries, static builds, and SGX enclaves are essentially identical.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT) architectures are attractive targets for side-channel enabled attackers due to their broader attack surface. This paper introduces a new side-channel technique called PORTSMASH that exploits port contention in SMT execution engines, specifically targeting Intel Skylake and Kaby Lake architectures with Hyper-Threading technology. The authors demonstrate an end-to-end attack by measuring the timing delays caused by port contention during ECDSA P-384 signature generation on a TLS server running OpenSSL 1.1.0h. They also show that this technique can be applied to SGX enclaves, indicating its wide applicability. The paper discusses the stealthiness of the attack and compares it with other side-channel techniques in terms of spatial resolution and detectability.
